videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Santiago de Chile, and in this video, we're going to show you our top things to do here. So hope you enjoy it! Santiago is the dynamic capital city of Chile, tucked in a valley between the Andes Mountains and the Pacific Ocean. This modern city boasts a thriving culinary scene, diverse neighborhoods, a vibrant nightlife, and a pleasant Mediterranean climate. On top of that, Santiago serves as the ideal starting point for Chilean adventures, offering easy transportation connections all around the country, from vineyards and beaches to national parks and canyons. In this video, we highlight 10 of the best things to do in and around Santiago de Chile. At the end, we'll also highlight a few travel tips. Now let's dive in! We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Santa Lucía hill is a small green oasis in the heart of Santiago. Once a fortress, this historic hill was transformed into a beautiful park offering panoramic views of the city. Ascend through its charming pathways adorned with flowers, fountains, and historical monuments. Explore the remains of the fortress walls and take in the views from the top. At the time of our visit, it was free to go to the park, but it does close in the evening. Santiago de Chile boasts a variety of museums, covering diverse fields and topics. We visited the Chilean Museum of Pre-Colombian Art, which houses an extensive collection of artifacts from across South America and Chile specifically. From intricately-carved stone sculptures to delicate textiles and gold objects, the museum offers a journey through the rich and diverse cultures that thrived before the arrival of Europeans. The Chile section is especially interesting as you'll learn about various indigenous groups that lived across the country. The Cajon del Maipo is a picturesque gorge located in the Andes Mountains, about 2 hours to the east of Santiago de Chile. This stunning landscape features towering cliffs, cascading waterfalls, and crystal-clear rivers. The El Yeso Dam takes center stage here, a reservoir that provides a significant portion of the drinking water supply for the city of Santiago. We visited the large artificial lake as a day trip from Santiago and made several stops at different viewpoints. The tour ended with a picnic-style lunch served with a glass of wine along the El Yeso River. Located in the high-end east side of Santiago, Parque Bicentenario is an expansive park that stretches along the Mapocho river. In this urban oasis, you'll find many lush green spaces, walking paths, a vibrant lake, and various recreational areas. Above all, it's scenic, well-maintained, and clean, with lots of picnic areas. It offers a welcoming escape from the bustling city and provides a tranquil setting for a relaxing afternoon. Santiago de Chile boasts a surprising number of vineyards within its city limits. Two notable examples are Santa Carolina and Cousiño Macul. We visited the latter vineyard, which is one of the oldest wineries in Chile. During a 1.5 hour tour we explored the winery's impressive cellars while indulging in wine tastings. We also learned about the wine-making process, the history of Chilean wine, and this family-owned vineyard. If you have more time, you can also venture beyond the city to nearby valleys like Casablanca, Maipo, or Colchagua. These offer a wider range of vineyards coupled with breathtaking scenery. Valparaíso and Viña del Mar are two coastal cities located about 120 km northwest of Santiago. Valparaíso is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and is known for its colorful hillside houses, beautiful street art, and vibrant port. Viña del Mar, on the other hand, is often called "the Garden City" and boasts stunning beaches and parks. We combined both of these cities in a day trip from Santiago. This tour included a scenic coastal drive, spotting sea lions, getting a guided tour of the artsy streets in Valparaíso, and taking a ride on traditional elevators to ascend the city's hills. Out of the the two cities we preferred Valparaíso since it seemed to have more character. But it was also a place that required awareness of our surroundings. The city is known to be a bit unsafe, so that's why we highly recommend to either do thorough research so you know where to go and where not to go, or get a tour guide and in that way you also learn a bit of history. The Sky Costanera, located atop the Gran Torre Santiago, is the tallest skyscraper in South America, reaching 300 meters or 984 feet in height. At the top, you'll find an observation deck with 360° vistas that stretch across the city as well as the Andes Mountain. There's also a bar that serves drinks and light meals. As it's open late at night, this is one of the best places in the city to watch the sunset. However, you should also keep in mind that golden hour is the busiest time to visit. Providencia is a vibrant and safe district in Santiago, known for its modern infrastructure, diverse culinary scene, and charming neighborhoods. Explore Barrio Italia, a haven for foodies. Stroll along its picturesque streets, admire the architecture, and enjoy some amazing cuisine. For a taste of Bohemian charm, venture into nearby Barrio Lastarría, known for its art galleries, antiques, and a vibrant nightlife scene with a variety of bars and restaurants. You can find a link to our interactive map with great places to eat and drink here in the description below this video. Cerro San Cristóbal is a prominent hill and largest green space in the heart of Santiago. It's accessed from various points in the city by funicular, cable car, hiking, or biking trails. The summit boasts a towering statue of the Virgin Mary and offers stunning vistas of the city below. Enjoy a leisurely stroll or sip on a traditional Chilean drink from one of the snack stands while taking in the views. Within its park limits, you can also find a zoo, botanical gardens, and public playgrounds. Centered around Plaza de Armas, the historic center of Santiago is a blend of Spanish colonial, neoclassical, and art déco architecture. Santiago has served as Chile's capital city since colonial times and experienced significant growth and development as well as challenges such as earthquakes and political instability. Some iconic landmarks include the San Francisco Church, the Stock Exchange Building, la Moneda Presidential Palace, and the Grand Cathedral of Santiago. If you want to delve deeper into the city's history and culture, we recommend going on a free walking tour. Our guide offered great insight on Chilean society, politics, and current events. As we promised at the beginning of the video, we'll now share a few travel and safety tips for Santiago de Chile. First, staying in areas like Providencia, Barrio Lastarría and Bellas Artes offers a good balance of safety, convenience, and access to restaurants, cafés, and cultural attractions. We stayed in a hotel in Barrio Yungay, which had a great price-quality but the downside was its safety concerns and lack of tourist facilities. However, if you're willing to take Ubers around it's a good option. Santiago has an excellent metro system, making it easy to navigate the city quickly and efficiently, especially during rush hour. Consider using Uber for shorter distances or areas not well served by the metro. Finally, while Santiago offers a lot to explore, it's crucial to be mindful of your surroundings. Research and be aware of areas that may have higher crime rates. At the time of our visit, some parts of Bellavista, the Historic Center, and the Central Market and bus station experienced some safety concerns. It's advisable to exercise caution, especially at night, and avoid walking alone in poorly lit areas. If you have more time in Santiago, there are also a few more interesting things to do. We put a list of those on the screen here for you. And if you're planning your trip to Chile, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below. We also included other important links in the description that will help with your Santiago experience, such as an interactive map so you can get to each place we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

people of all backgrounds and beliefs are welcome to meditate pray and learn about the Baha'i faith one of the main attractions of the temple is its unique design which combines elements of traditional Baha'i architecture with modern influences the nine sides of the temple represent the unity of the world's religions and the surrounding Gardens and water features create a peaceful and Serene atmosphere it is a must-see destination for anyone interested in spirituality architecture or cultural Exchange number nine take in Santiago's Plaza de armas located in the Heart of the City the plaza de armas is a bustling Hub of activity and a great place to people watch and soak up the local culture one of the highlights of the plaza de armas is the elegant colonial style architecture that surrounds it the plaza is home to a number of historic buildings including the National History Museum the central post office and the National Library these buildings are adorned with stunning neoclassical details and are worth a visit on their own the plaza is also home to a number of street performers vendors and Artisans who add to the Lively atmosphere of the plaza you'll find everything from musicians and dancers to painters and craftspeople showcasing their talents number eight see the Santiago Metropolitan Cathedral the Santiago Metropolitan Cathedral is an impressive and historic religious Building located in the Heart of the City the cathedral which dates back to the 18th century is a stunning example of neoclassical architecture it is adorned with exquisite marble details ornate frescoes and intricate stained glass windows the interior of the cathedral is equally impressive with a grandiose altar beautiful chapels and a richly decorated Dome the Santiago Metropolitan Cathedral is also a significant cultural and historical site it is the seat of the Archbishop of Santiago and has played a central role in the city's spiritual and cultural life force entries number seven eat local food at the Central Market eating local food at the Central Market in Santiago is a great way to immerse yourself in the culture and traditions of Chile the market is a bustling Hub of activity filled with fresh locally grown produce and ingredients as well as a variety of traditional Chilean dishes by eating local food at the Central Market you'll have the opportunity to try new and unique flavors and ingredients that you may not find at other restaurants additionally by supporting local vendors and small businesses you'll be contributing to the local economy and helping to sustain the community plus the Central Market is a great place to meet locals and learn more about their culture and way of life by the way if you want to learn how to save money on airfares then click the link in the description below this guide will teach you how to always get the cheapest flights number six see the changing of the guards ceremony Palacio de la moneda is the official residence of the President of Chile and is a popular tourist attraction in Santiago visit can watch the changing of the guards a ceremony that takes place daily at noon and admire the Magnificent neoclassical architecture of the palace the ceremony is a traditional military tradition that dates back to the early 19th century and it is a spectacle to behold the guards are dressed in traditional military uniforms and perform precise drills and Maneuvers as they change places the ceremony is accompanied by a military band and plays a selection of traditional Chilean music number five visit Pablo neruda's house Pablo Neruda was a renowned Chilean poet Diplomat and political activist who is considered one of the greatest Poets of the 20th century his house known as lachaskona is located in the bayavista neighborhood of Santiago and was one of three homes that Naruto built for himself and his Muse the chescona is a stunning example of naruta's creativity and taste with a blend of styles and influences from traditional Chilean European and Asian elements it's filled with his personal collection of art books and objects from around the world lechascona is also a testament to naruta's love for his country because it is decorated with Chilean folk art and crafts it has been preserved as a museum and is open to the public allowing visitors to explore the poet's private spaces and learn more about his life and work number four take a cable car UPS on Cristobal Hill San Cristobal Hill is a popular tourist destination that offers breathtaking panoramic views of Santiago and the surrounding area the cable car ride up the hill is a thrilling and Scenic experience in itself and once you reach the top you'll be rewarded with stunning views of the city and the Andes Mountains there are also several attractions at the top of the hill including a statue of the Virgin Mary a chapel and a wildlife sanctuary where you can see llamas foxes and other native animals number three check out the mesmerizing Santiago Cemetery the Santiago General Cemetery might not seem like a typical tourist destination but it's actually a fascinating and mesmerizing place to visit it houses a wide variety of stunning architectural Styles including Art Deco our Nouveau neoclassical and Gothic the cemetery houses the graves of some of Chile's most famous and influential figures including writers politicians and military leaders the memorial of salvadoria Allende who was ousted as President of Chile in 1973 in a coup is perhaps the most visited visitors can take a guided tour of the cemetery to learn more about the history and culture of Santiago or simply wander around and admire the beautiful architecture and peaceful surroundings number two visit the highest observation point in South America the sky costanetta is an observation deck located on the top floor of the grand Torre Santiago the tallest building in Latin America visiting sky costanera is an opportunity to experience the modern side of Santiago and get a unique perspective on the city from above from here you can see all the way to the Andes Mountains on a clear day and taken the city's bustling streets and towering skyscrapers the building itself is a feat of modern architecture and engineering and the observation deck offers 360 degree views of the surrounding cityscape number one tour to Cajon Del maipo located just outside of Santiago the Cajon Del maipo is known for its rugged Mountain Landscapes Crystal Clear rivers and beautiful valleys one of the main attractions in Cajon Del maipo is the opportunity to go hiking or horseback riding through the stunning landscape there are a variety of trails to choose from ranging from easy to challenging so there's something for everyone the scenery is breathtaking with snow-capped mountains cascading waterfalls and Lush Forest providing the perfect backdrop for your adventure Cajon Del maipo is also home to a number of charming small towns and Villages that offer a glimpse into traditional Chilean culture here you'll find artisanal crafts locally produced food and wine and friendly locals who were happy to share their their way of life with visitors click here for more tips while traveling in Chile

videos.transcript: Hi, I am Martijn, your guide in Santiago de Chile. Nestled in the Andes mountains. I am three days in Santiago de Chile to show you the best things to do and see. From beautiful viewing points to must-sea museums and everything in between. If this Santiago de Chile travel guide has given you new travel tips or maybe you just like it, please give a thumbs up or leave a comment under the video. That's the way you can help so other people can discover the video as well and I get to create new travel guides for you in the future. The city is served by Santiago International Airport. And to travel to the city, you can use, of course, a taxi service. My advice is to look for the authorized taxis and agree on the fare before you leave. There's also an Uber taxi service. And what I saw in my app, the prices are between €14 and €25. And there is also a bus service, Centro Puerto and Turbus. And my advice is to look for the timets before you come. Let's start the Santiago de Chile travel guide in the heart of the city center on Plaza de Armas. This big square dates back to 1541 when the city was founded by Pedro de Valdivia. It was the core of the city's layouts during the Spanish colonial period which lasted for approximately 300 years. You'll find the statue of Pedro over here and besides that a lot of nice buildings. For example, the Metropolitan Cathedral. It's a symbol of resilience. Its history begins in the mid 16th century when the first church was built on a very spot. Over the centuries, earthquakes destroyed earlier structures. But each time the cathedral rose again stronger and more magnificent than before. The current cathedral completed during the 18th and 19th centuries blends neo-classical beauty with baroque details. You will also find the central post office over here and that's a fine example of neo-classical architecture. And the city's town hall. It showcases a beautiful combination of neo-classical and renaissance architecture. In a moment you know more about a great local market to visit... ...and a must-see museum. But first we are going skyhigh to the top of a hill. Cerro san Cristobal. You can hike, bike, take a finicular, cable car or a bus service to the top. Of course, you have a breathtaking stunning view around the city and maybe you see the Andes mountains as well. On the summit of the hill stands one of the city's most beloved symbols, the towering statue of the Virgin Mary. Completed in 1908, this 14 meter high monument watches over the capital as a bacon of peace and protection. At its base lies a small chapel, a quiet space where you can pause to reflect and pray. The city is full with a lot of bustling markets and the most famous one is Mercado Central. Well known for the great seafood, restaurants and a vibrant atmosphere. And it's one of the most traditional markets in the city and it's dating back to 1872. The structure was designed by Gustaf Eiffel, the same engineer as the Eiffel Tower in Paris. And it's mostly fully packed during lunchtime and in the weekends. And keep in mind that some restaurants only accept cash. In a moment, you know, another great viewing point, but first, the Museum of Memory and Human Rights. The museum is dedicated to show respect for the victims of human right violations during the Pinochet regime. It reversed to the period of military rule in Chile led by General Augusto Pinochet. It began with a coupe on September the 11th, 1973 and the regime lasted until 1990. The museum features a range of exhibits including photographs, documents, testimonies, and a memorial wall. It's your lucky day because it's time for a nice exercise to go to the top of the Santa Lucia Hill. It's located in the heart of the city center and it's beautiful landscaped with gardens... ....sculptures and fountains. And that makes it a peaceful oasis in the heart of this bustling city. The Santa Lucua has a rich historical background. It was originally called Huelén... ...and later it served as a military fort during the Spanish colonial period. The hill offers well-maintained trails and stairs to the top. The only thing you have to bring with you are comfortable walking shoes. And when you are finally on the top, you will have a stunning view around the city. In a moment, you know a colorful neighborhood to visit. But first, the presidential palace of Chile. It's located in the city, La Moneda. It was built in the late 18th century and played a major role in the history of the country. One of the most notable events was the military coupe in September 1973 when the palace was bombed. Which led to the overthrown of President Allende. Nowadays, the palace also houses a cultural center with, for example, art exhibitions... ...and you can watch the changing of the guards, which is on every other day at 10:00 in the morning and on Saturday at 11:00. A lively neighborhood you certainly have to visit is Barrio Bellavista. It's well known for its bohemian atmosphere because of the art galleries, restaurants, boutiques, and its vibrant night life. My advice is to stroll around over here and enjoy all the colors, restaurants, and art. You will also find La Chascona in this neighborhood. This is a museum dedicated to the life and work of the Chilean poet writer Pablo Neruda. It's time for some greenery in Parque Forestal. It's a big urban park in the city center. It was built in the early 20th century and a perfect place to stroll around to jog, sport, or maybe relax. The park is home to some cultural institutions, for example, the Museum of Fine Arts. This museum dates back to 1880 and is one of the leading art museums in Chile. There are two more neighborhoods which I want to show you in this video. And the first one is Barrio Lastarria. It's a kind of bohemian neighborhood in the city. Well known for the art galleries, the cafes, boutiques, and the street markets. My advice is to walk around over here and enjoy the local culture. And the other neighborhood I want to show you is Barrio Italia. It's a perfect place for a cup of coffee and check out all the art galleries, boutiques, and restaurants. What is the best time for a visit? I think the period between November and May. That's the spring, summer, and also the fall season. The city is well known for a lot of sunshine.
